facts.text: Little Ferry was named after the rope‑drawn ferry that crossed the Hackensack River between 1659 and 1826.
The borough’s first train station, built in 1875 by the New York and Long Branch Railroad, earned a spot on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984.
Early Little Ferry was a lively mix of resort, farm, and fishing town before evolving into a residential community.
When NJ Transit trains stop at the historic station, they block one of two grade crossings, often causing brief traffic backups.
The original Parker Homestead, dating to 1725, is one of New Jersey’s oldest houses and is now undergoing renovation by the borough.
Today Little Ferry’s streets are lined with a variety of housing styles, from ranches to capes, reflecting its transition from farms and nurseries to a primarily residential area.
